behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real recursive filesystem watching plus daemon pipeline handoff. MOCK-HIDE applies: synthetic watcher callbacks can miss OS-specific recursion gaps. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text requires real watch behavior for subdirectories under the watched root.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against real temp input/output dirs. Create a nested subdirectory under watched root, write a file into it, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure is module handoff (request intake -> dedup store/windowing -> enqueue decision). Real OS behavior is not required if requests are injected in-process with controlled clock/window config. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network/subprocess needed, no AI dependency, no multi-agentic chain. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text is about duplicate request suppression by path/time window, not OS event semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake + dedup + queue modules with real in-memory state and configurable window. Submit same file path twice within window, assert second is dropped; submit again after window, assert accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS event duplication; this is a real infrastructure boundary. MOCK-HIDE applies strongly: mocked event streams may not reproduce timing/coalescing edge cases that trigger duplicate processing.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon with real watcher on temp dir. Perform one physical write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename or rapid append/close). Assert pipeline runs once for that path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Requires real OS filesystem lifecycle (delete/recreate, inode changes, watcher reattachment). In-process mocks can hide re-subscription failures tied to actual watcher backends. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly requires live remove/recreate while running.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on real temp watched dir. Remove directory, recreate it at same path, then add a new file. Assert daemon detects and processes the new file without process restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on real file metadata acquisition and near-simultaneous arrival under watcher scheduling. Integration can test sorter logic, but real failure often appears at filesystem timestamp granularity + event/order handoff. REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because behavior text binds ordering to creation timestamps of arriving files, which are OS-provided metadata.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on real dirs, create multiple files in rapid succession with controlled creation times (or staged writes preserving distinct creation order). Assert processing sequence matches creation timestamp ordering.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This failure emerges from real write-in-progress behavior, event timing, and file-stability checks. MOCK-HIDE applies: fake events or fake FS usually cannot reproduce partial-write timing races accurately.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon and begin a slow/streamed write to a file in watched dir. Verify daemon does not process during partial write, then closes writer and verify processing starts afterward exactly once.
